owasp_api_security: API3:2023 - Broken Object Property Level Authorization
cwe: CWE-915 - Improperly Controlled Modification of Dynamically-Determined Object Attributes

threat.title: Mass Assignment Vulnerability Leading to Privilege Escalation in Spring Boot and Django APIs
threat.description: Mass assignment (also known as over-posting or auto-binding) occurs when APIs automatically bind HTTP request parameters to internal object properties without proper filtering. Attackers can modify sensitive fields like user roles, permissions, account balances, or administrative flags that should never be user-controllable. This vulnerability is particularly dangerous in Spring Boot's @ModelAttribute/@RequestBody binding and Django's ModelForm processing, where framework conveniences can inadvertently expose critical attributes to manipulation.

attack.scenario:
1. Attacker discovers API endpoint accepting user profile updates (e.g., PUT /api/users/123)
2. Inspects legitimate request body and identifies modifiable fields: {"name":"John","email":"john@example.com"}
3. Reviews application source code, database schema, or uses parameter fuzzing to discover hidden fields (e.g., isAdmin, role, accountBalance, permissions)
4. Crafts malicious request adding unauthorized fields: {"name":"John","email":"john@example.com","isAdmin":true,"role":"ADMIN"}
5. Framework automatically binds all provided parameters to the User object without validation
6. Database persists the modified values, granting attacker administrative privileges or manipulating sensitive data
7. Attacker exploits elevated privileges to access restricted resources, exfiltrate data, or modify critical system settings
prerequisites: API endpoint accepts object binding via @ModelAttribute, @RequestBody (Spring) or ModelForm, Serializer (Django)
Sensitive object properties lack @JsonIgnore, exclude, or read_only protection
Application relies on framework auto-binding without manual validation
Attacker has authenticated access (low-privilege account sufficient)

cve_references.description: Mass assignment vulnerability in Ruby on Rails that led to GitHub breach where attacker added SSH key to Rails organization
cve_references.framework: Ruby on Rails (similar pattern applies to Spring/Django)

cve_references.description: Spring Framework Data Binding Rules vulnerability - case-sensitive disallowedFields patterns failed to protect fields, enabling privilege escalation
Spring4Shell RCE via data binding on JDK 9+ - demonstrates risks of unrestricted parameter binding to internal objects

real_world_breaches.impact: Security researcher Egor Homakov exploited mass assignment in Ruby on Rails to add his SSH public key to the Rails organization repository, gaining unauthorized commit access. Incident led to Rails implementing Strong Parameters.
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Unprotected mass assignment in public key update form allowed modification of organization membership via public_key[user_id] parameter

real_world_breaches.impact: CVE-2022-22968 discovered where case-sensitive field filtering in DataBinder allowed attackers to bypass protection by changing case of first character, potentially escalating privileges
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Spring's DataBinder disallowedFields patterns were case-sensitive, requiring both 'isAdmin' and 'IsAdmin' to be blocked

bug_bounty_reports.summary: Mass assignment vulnerabilities consistently rank in top API security findings on bug bounty platforms, with privilege escalation via role/permission modification being most common

security_research.title: OWASP API Security Top 10 2023 - API3:2023 Broken Object Property Level Authorization
security_research.key_finding: Mass Assignment and Excessive Data Exposure merged into API3:2023, emphasizing that allowing clients to modify or read object properties without proper authorization is a critical API vulnerability

security_research.title: Hunting For Mass Assignment Vulnerabilities Using GitHub CodeSearch
security_research.key_finding: Systematic analysis of public repositories reveals mass assignment vulnerabilities remain prevalent in modern applications despite being well-documented for over a decade

mitigations.title: Spring Boot 3.x: Use DTOs with Explicit Field Mapping and @JsonIgnore
mitigations.description: Implement Data Transfer Objects (DTOs) that expose only user-modifiable fields. Use @JsonIgnore, @JsonIgnoreProperties, or custom deserialization to protect sensitive properties. This approach provides compile-time safety and explicit control over API surface.

tools.name: MapStruct
tools.usage: Compile-time safe bean mapping for DTO <-> Entity conversion

tools.name: ModelMapper
tools.usage: Runtime object mapping with field exclusion support

validation_steps: Create unit tests attempting to modify sensitive fields via API
Verify @JsonIgnore prevents field inclusion in JSON responses
Code review: Ensure no @Entity classes used directly as @RequestBody
Fuzz test API with unexpected parameters (isAdmin, role, etc.)

mitigations.title: Django: Use ModelForm with Explicit fields and read_only_fields
mitigations.description: Configure Django ModelForms and Django REST Framework Serializers with explicit 'fields' allowlists instead of '__all__' or 'exclude'. Mark sensitive fields as read_only to prevent modification via API requests.

tools.name: django-guardian
tools.usage: Fine-grained permission control for object attributes

validation_steps: Test API with {'is_admin': True} in request body - should be ignored
Verify read_only_fields cannot be modified via POST/PUT/PATCH
Unit test: Attempt role escalation via API should fail
Review all ModelForms/Serializers: No fields='__all__' or exclude patterns

mitigations.title: General API Design: Implement Field-Level Authorization and Input Validation
mitigations.description: Apply defense-in-depth with explicit authorization checks, input validation schemas (JSON Schema, OpenAPI), and monitoring for unexpected parameters. Use API gateways to enforce request validation before reaching application logic.

code_example.code:
# OpenAPI 3.0 Schema - Strict Request Validation
openapi: 3.0.0
info:
  title: User Management API
  version: 1.0.0

paths:
  /api/users/{userId}:
    put:
      summary: Update user profile
      parameters:
        - name: userId
          in: path
          required: true
          schema:
            type: integer
      requestBody:
        required: true
        content:
          application/json:
            schema:
              $ref: '#/components/schemas/UserUpdateRequest'
      responses:
        '200':
          description: User updated successfully
        '400':
          description: Invalid request - unexpected fields provided
        '403':
          description: Forbidden - cannot modify other user's profile

components:
  schemas:
    UserUpdateRequest:
      type: object
      required:
        - username
        - email
      properties:
        username:
          type: string
          minLength: 3
          maxLength: 50
          pattern: '^[a-zA-Z0-9_-]+$'
        email:
          type: string
          format: email
        bio:
          type: string
          maxLength: 500
      additionalProperties: false  # ✅ CRITICAL: Reject requests with unexpected fields

---
# Azure API Management Policy - Request Validation
<policies>
    <inbound>
        <base />
        <!-- Validate request against OpenAPI schema -->
        <validate-content unspecified-content-type-action="prevent" 
                          max-size="102400" 
                          size-exceeded-action="detect" 
                          errors-variable-name="requestBodyValidation">
            <content type="application/json" 
                     validate-as="json" 
                     action="prevent" 
                     allow-additional-properties="false" />
        </validate-content>
        
        <!-- Block requests containing sensitive fields -->
        <choose>
            <when condition="@(context.Request.Body.As<JObject>(preserveContent: true).Properties().Any(p => 
                new[] {"isAdmin", "role", "accountBalance", "is_admin", "permissions", "created_at"}.Contains(p.Name)))">
                <return-response>
                    <set-status code="400" reason="Bad Request" />
                    <set-body>{
                        "error": "Request contains forbidden fields",
                        "message": "Cannot modify admin, role, or system fields"
                    }</set-body>
                </return-response>
            </when>
        </choose>
        
        <!-- Rate limiting to prevent mass assignment fuzzing attacks -->
        <rate-limit calls="20" renewal-period="60" 
                    remaining-calls-variable-name="remainingCallsPerSubscription" />
    </inbound>
    <backend>
        <base />
    </backend>
    <outbound>
        <base />
        <!-- Remove sensitive fields from responses -->
        <set-body>@{
            var response = context.Response.Body.As<JObject>(preserveContent: true);
            response.Remove("accountBalance");
            response.Remove("isAdmin");
            response.Remove("role");
            return response.ToString();
        }</set-body>
    </outbound>
    <on-error>
        <base />
    </on-error>
</policies>

---
# Python: JSON Schema Validation Middleware (Framework-agnostic)
import jsonschema
from jsonschema import validate, ValidationError
from functools import wraps

USER_UPDATE_SCHEMA = {
    "type": "object",
    "properties": {
        "username": {"type": "string", "minLength": 3, "maxLength": 50},
        "email": {"type": "string", "format": "email"},
        "bio": {"type": "string", "maxLength": 500}
    },
    "required": ["username", "email"],
    "additionalProperties": False  # Reject unexpected fields
}

def validate_request_schema(schema):
    """Decorator to validate request body against JSON schema"""
    def decorator(f):
        @wraps(f)
        def wrapper(*args, **kwargs):
            request_body = request.get_json()
            try:
                validate(instance=request_body, schema=schema)
            except ValidationError as e:
                return jsonify({
                    'error': 'Invalid request',
                    'message': str(e.message),
                    'path': list(e.path)
                }), 400
            return f(*args, **kwargs)
        return wrapper
    return decorator

# Usage
@app.route('/api/users/<int:user_id>', methods=['PUT'])
@login_required
@validate_request_schema(USER_UPDATE_SCHEMA)
def update_user(user_id):
    # Request already validated - proceed with update
    pass

tools.name: Azure API Management
tools.usage: Request validation, field filtering, rate limiting

tools.name: Amazon API Gateway
tools.usage: OpenAPI schema validation with request validators

nist_csf: DE.CM-1 - Network monitored for anomalous activity
validation_steps: Deploy OpenAPI schema with additionalProperties: false
Test API with unexpected fields - should return 400 Bad Request
Verify API gateway blocks requests with sensitive fields
Monitor logs for mass assignment attempt patterns
Penetration test: Fuzz API with common sensitive field names

indicators: HTTP 200 responses for requests containing unexpected field names (isAdmin, role, permissions)
Sudden privilege escalation events (user role changed from USER to ADMIN)
Account balance modifications without corresponding transaction records
User objects with recently modified sensitive fields (created_at, is_active, role)
Multiple failed authorization checks followed by successful access with elevated privileges
API requests with large JSON payloads containing 10+ unusual parameter names

log_queries.query:
// Detect mass assignment attempts with sensitive fields
AzureDiagnostics
| where Category == "ApplicationGatewayAccessLog" or Category == "FunctionAppLogs"
| where requestUri_s contains "/api/users/"
| where httpMethod_s in ("PUT", "PATCH", "POST")
| extend requestBody = parse_json(requestBody_s)
| where requestBody has_any ("isAdmin", "role", "is_admin", "permissions", "accountBalance", "account_balance", "is_active")
| summarize AttemptCount = count(), 
            DistinctIPs = dcount(clientIP_s),
            SuspiciousFields = make_set(dynamic_to_json(bag_keys(requestBody)))
          by userIdentity_s, bin(TimeGenerated, 5m)
| where AttemptCount > 3
| order by TimeGenerated desc

// Detect privilege escalation via mass assignment
event.category: "web" AND http.request.method: (PUT OR PATCH OR POST) 
AND url.path: /api/users/* 
AND http.request.body.content: (*isAdmin* OR *role* OR *permissions* OR *accountBalance* OR *is_active*)
AND NOT user.roles: "admin"
| stats count by user.id, source.ip, event.outcome, span(5m)
| where count > 2

index=app_logs sourcetype=api_access 
(uri_path="/api/users/*" OR uri_path="/api/profiles/*") 
http_method IN ("PUT", "PATCH", "POST")
| rex field=request_body "(?<suspicious_field>isAdmin|role|permissions|accountBalance|is_active|is_admin|account_balance)" 
| search suspicious_field=*
| stats count as attempts, 
        values(suspicious_field) as fields_attempted, 
        dc(client_ip) as unique_ips,
        values(response_status) as status_codes
  by user_id, _time span=5m
| where attempts > 3 OR unique_ips > 2
| eval severity=case(
    status_codes="200", "CRITICAL",
    attempts > 5, "HIGH",
    1=1, "MEDIUM"
  )
| table _time, user_id, attempts, fields_attempted, unique_ips, status_codes, severity
| sort -_time

// Detect successful privilege escalation
requests
| where url contains "/api/users/"
| where success == true
| where customDimensions has_any ("isAdmin", "role", "permissions")
| join kind=inner (
    traces
    | where message contains "User role updated" or message contains "Privilege escalation"
  ) on operation_Id
| project timestamp, user_AuthenticatedId, url, 
          resultCode, customDimensions, message
| order by timestamp desc
